verses.commentary: Therefore, there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus. The glorious therefore responds to chapter 7's cry. No condemnation (katakrima, κατάκριμα) means no judicial verdict of guilt. This is absolute: no condemnation, now or ever. The ground: being in Christ Jesus. Union with Christ changes everything.

Because through Christ Jesus the law of the Spirit who gives life has set you free from the law of sin and death. The law of the Spirit is the principle of the Spirit's life-giving power. The law of sin and death is the principle of sin's deadly dominion (7:23). Liberation comes through Christ Jesus. The Spirit's power breaks sin's power.

For what the law was powerless to do because it was weakened by the flesh, God did by sending His own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh to be a sin offering. And so He condemned sin in the flesh. The law could not save because we are weak. God did what the law could not. His own Son emphasizes the cost. Likeness of sinful flesh: Jesus was truly human but without sin. Sin offering (peri hamartias, περὶ ἁμαρτίας) echoes Leviticus. God condemned sin in the flesh through Christ's death. The penalty was paid in Christ's body.

In order that the righteous requirement of the law might be fully met in us, who do not live according to the flesh but according to the Spirit. The law's requirement is fulfilled in us, not by us. Christ met the law's demands. We live according to the Spirit, not the flesh. The Spirit enables what the law commanded but could not produce.

Those who live according to the flesh have their minds set on what the flesh desires; but those who live in accordance with the Spirit have their minds set on what the Spirit desires. Two orientations: flesh or Spirit. The mind's focus reveals the heart's allegiance. Flesh-minded people pursue fleshly things; Spirit-led people pursue spiritual things.

The mind governed by the flesh is death, but the mind governed by the Spirit is life and peace. Two outcomes: death or life. The mind (phronēma, φρόνημα, mindset, way of thinking) determines destiny. Flesh produces death; Spirit produces life and peace (shalom, wholeness).

The mind governed by the flesh is hostile to God; it does not submit to God's law, nor can it do so. Flesh-mindedness is active hostility (echthra, ἔχθρα, enmity) toward God. It will not and cannot submit. This is total inability. Apart from the Spirit, submission to God is impossible.

Those who are in the realm of the flesh cannot please God. Absolute inability. Those dominated by the flesh (unregenerate) cannot please God. Whatever they do, apart from faith and the Spirit, falls short. Pleasing God requires regeneration.

You, however, are not in the realm of the flesh but are in the realm of the Spirit, if indeed the Spirit of God lives in you. And if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, they do not belong to Christ. Transition from flesh to Spirit is definitive. If introduces not doubt but the condition: having the Spirit. The Spirit's indwelling is the mark of belonging to Christ. No Spirit, no Christianity. The Spirit of God and Spirit of Christ are used interchangeably, affirming the Trinity.

But if Christ is in you, then even though your body is subject to death because of sin, the Spirit gives life because of righteousness. Christ in you (union with Christ) is the same as the Spirit in you. The body still dies (physical death is the result of sin). But the Spirit gives life: spiritual life now, resurrection life later. Righteousness is the ground of the Spirit's life-giving work.

And if the Spirit of Him who raised Jesus from the dead is living in you, He who raised Christ from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies because of His Spirit who lives in you. The same Spirit who raised Jesus will raise us. Resurrection is guaranteed by the indwelling Spirit. Mortal bodies will be made immortal. The Spirit is the firstfruits (v. 23) and guarantee of final redemption.

Therefore, brothers and sisters, we have an obligation, but it is not to the flesh, to live according to it. We owe the flesh nothing. It enslaved us; Christ freed us. Obligation is to the Spirit, not the flesh.

For if you live according to the flesh, you will die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the misdeeds of the body, you will live. Two paths: flesh or Spirit; death or life. Living according to the flesh leads to spiritual death. Putting to death the deeds of the body is mortification: active killing of sin. This is done by the Spirit, not human effort. The result is life.

For those who are led by the Spirit of God are the children of God. Being led by the Spirit is the mark of God's children. This is not sinless perfection but a pattern of life. Children of God (huioi theou, υἱοἱ θεοῦ) emphasizes sonship, not just creation.

The Spirit you received does not make you slaves, so that you live in fear again; rather, the Spirit you received brought about your adoption to sonship. And by Him we cry, Abba, Father. The Spirit does not enslave but adopts. Adoption (huiothesia, υἱοθεσία) confers full legal rights as sons. Abba (Aramaic) and Father (Greek) together express intimate relationship. Crying Abba is the Spirit's testimony in us. We are children, not slaves.

The Spirit Himself testifies with our spirit that we are God's children. The Spirit witnesses to our sonship. This is internal assurance. The Spirit confirms what God declares. Our spirit and the Spirit bear joint testimony. This is the ground of Christian assurance.

Now if we are children, then we are heirs, heirs of God and co-heirs with Christ, if indeed we share in His sufferings in order that we may also share in His glory. Sonship means heirship. We inherit with Christ. Co-heirs (synklēronomoi, συγκληρονόμοι) means equal inheritance. Suffering precedes glory. Sharing Christ's sufferings qualifies us to share His glory. This is the already-not yet: suffering now, glory later.

I consider that our present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory that will be revealed in us. Paul's evaluation: present suffering is light compared to coming glory (2 Corinthians 4:17). Glory will be revealed in us, not just to us. We will be glorified, not just spectators of glory.

For the creation waits in eager expectation for the children of God to be revealed. All creation (ktisis, κτίσις, the natural world) waits eagerly. Eager expectation (apokaradokia, ἀποκαραδοκία) suggests intense longing, head outstretched. Creation longs for the revelation of God's sons because it will share in their liberation.

For the creation was subjected to frustration, not by its own choice, but by the will of the one who subjected it, in hope. Frustration (mataiotēs, ματαιότης) means futility, emptiness. Creation was subjected to this by God (consequence of Adam's sin, Genesis 3:17-18). But it was subjected in hope. The curse is not permanent.

That the creation itself will be liberated from its bondage to decay and brought into the freedom and glory of the children of God. Creation will be freed from decay (phthora, φθορά, corruption). It will share in the glorious freedom of God's children. New heavens and new earth (Revelation 21:1) fulfill this promise. Redemption is cosmic, not just personal.

We know that the whole creation has been groaning as in the pains of childbirth right up to the present time. Groaning (systenazō, συστενάζω) describes collective suffering. Childbirth pains imply something new is coming. The groaning is purposeful, not meaningless. Creation travails, awaiting redemption.

Not only so, but we ourselves, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for our adoption to sonship, the redemption of our bodies. Believers also groan. The firstfruits of the Spirit is the down payment, guaranteeing full harvest. We groan for full adoption: bodily resurrection. Redemption includes the body, not escape from it. We await physical transformation.

For in this hope we were saved. But hope that is seen is no hope at all. Who hopes for what they already have? Salvation is already-not yet. We are saved (past), but full salvation is future. Hope is confident expectation of what is not yet seen. Seeing eliminates the need for hope.

But if we hope for what we do not yet have, we wait for it patiently. Hope produces patient endurance (hypomonē, ὑπομονή). Waiting is active, not passive. We endure present suffering in light of future glory.

In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness. We do not know what we ought to pray for, but the Spirit Himself intercedes for us through wordless groans. The Spirit helps (synantilambanomai, συναντιλαμβάνομαι, takes hold together with us) in our weakness. We don't know how to pray as we should. The Spirit intercedes with groans too deep for words. The Spirit prays in us and for us.

And He who searches our hearts knows the mind of the Spirit, because the Spirit intercedes for God's people in accordance with the will of God. God searches hearts and knows the Spirit's mind. The Spirit's intercession aligns with God's will. This guarantees that our prayers, even when inarticulate, are heard and answered according to God's perfect plan.

And we know that in all things God works for the good of those who love Him, who have been called according to His purpose. The promise: God works all things together for good. Not that all things are good, but God uses all things for good. The beneficiaries: those who love God and are called. Called implies divine initiative. Good is defined by God's purpose, not our comfort.

For those God foreknew He also predestined to be conformed to the image of His Son, that He might be the firstborn among many brothers and sisters. Foreknowledge implies personal, relational knowing, not just intellectual awareness. Predestination is God's determination of the end: conformity to Christ's image. The goal: Christ as firstborn among many siblings. We become like Jesus.

And those He predestined, He also called; those He called, He also justified; those He justified, He also glorified. The golden chain of salvation. Past tense throughout, even glorified (already accomplished in God's decree). The order: predestination, calling, justification, glorification. Nothing breaks the chain. Those God predestines are certainly glorified.

What, then, shall we say in response to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us? Paul draws conclusions. If God is for us, all opposition is overcome. This is not that no one opposes, but that no opposition succeeds. God's favor guarantees victory.

He who did not spare His own Son, but gave Him up for us all, how will He not also, along with Him, graciously give us all things? The argument from greater to lesser. If God gave His Son (the greatest gift), He will give everything else. Graciously emphasizes grace. All things means everything needed for salvation and godliness.

Who will bring any charge against those whom God has chosen? It is God who justifies. A courtroom scene. Who can accuse? No one, because God justifies. If the Judge declares righteous, no accusation stands. God's verdict silences all accusers.

Who then is the one who condemns? No one. Christ Jesus who died, more than that, who was raised to life, is at the right hand of God and is also interceding for us. Who condemns? No one. Christ died (atonement), was raised (vindication), is at God's right hand (exaltation), and intercedes for us (ongoing advocacy). Our defense lawyer is the Judge's Son. Condemnation is impossible.

Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall trouble or hardship or persecution or famine or nakedness or danger or sword? A list of trials. Can any of these separate us from Christ's love? The implied answer: No. External circumstances cannot sever the love bond.

As it is written: For Your sake we face death all day long; we are considered as sheep to be slaughtered (Psalm 44:22). Suffering is expected, not surprising. Sheep to be slaughtered depicts vulnerability. Yet even in this, we are not abandoned.

No, in all these things we are more than conquerors through Him who loved us. More than conquerors (hypernikaō, ὑπερνικάω, super-victors). We don't just survive; we triumph. Victory comes through Him who loved us. Christ's love is the ground of our conquest.

For I am convinced that neither death nor life, neither angels nor demons, neither the present nor the future, nor any powers. Convinced (peithō, πείθω, persuaded) expresses certainty. A comprehensive list: death, life, angels, demons, present, future, powers. Nothing in existence can separate.

Neither height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord. The list concludes: height, depth, anything else in all creation. Absolutely nothing can separate us from God's love in Christ Jesus. This is the believer's unshakable security. The love of God is in Christ Jesus our Lord. Union with Christ guarantees eternal union with God's love.
